text: Classes and Castes. The three main social classes are the working class, the middle class, and the upper class, but the boundaries between these groups are a matter of debate. The wealthiest 5 to 10 percent are usually regarded as upper class, with their wealth derived from ownership and control of property and capital. The growing middle class is defined as individuals with nonmanual occupations. Nonmanual workers typically earn more than manual workers, although upper-level manual workers such as tradespeople earn more than those in sales and personal service positions. The professions, which include such occupations as accountants, computing specialists, engineers, and medical doctors, have been one of the fastest growing sectors of the economy. Since the 1980s the number of manual workers has been in decline. Manual workers form the nucleus of the working class; 20 to 40 percent self-identify with this category. Class consciousness includes the acknowledgment of class divisions, but there is also a broad commitment to an ethic of egalitarianism. Australians commonly believe that socioeconomic mobility is possible and exhibit a basic tolerance and acceptance of inequality associated with social class. Symbols of Social Stratification. The upper-class can be signified by expensive clothes, motor vehicles, and homes. In particular, the economic value of housing and other real estate properties varies greatly across different suburbs in all cities. However, class is not always evident from clothes, cars, and living circumstances. Middle-class people from economically wealthy backgrounds Patterns of speech, consumption patterns associated with entertainment and the arts, and participation in certain sports may be useful indicators of class.

Classes and Castes. "Brazil is no longer an underdeveloped country. It is an unjust country," Brazilian President Fernando Henrique Cardoso proclaimed in 1994. Today Brazil, although one of the ten largest economies in the world, has the most unequal distribution of income of any nation except South Africa. Moreover, inequality has been growing. In the mid-1990s, the poorest 20 percent of the population received only 3 percent of national income, while the richest 10 percent received 47 percent. Or, put in another way, the wealthiest 20 percent earn twenty-six times as much as the poorest 20 percent. It is estimated that some thirty-three million Brazilians live in poverty, including twenty million workers and ten million pensioners who receive the minimum wage of around $115 a month. In parts of Brazil, particularly the Northeast, infant mortality, a sensitive indicator of social inequality, has actually been rising. This "social question," as Brazilians call the divide between rich and poor, has characterized the nation since colonial times. With industrialization and urbanization during the first decades of the twentieth century, however, the growth of the Brazilian middle class has made this simple division more complex. Today, depending on how it is defined, the middle class accounts for one-fifth to one-third of the population, but the resources and lifestyle of its members vary considerably. Some claim the Brazilian middle class admires elite values and aspires to elite status and it is indeed true that middle-class families in Brazil are far more likely to employ domestic servants and send their children to private school than their North American counterparts. Still, a ray of hope emerged with the stabilization of the Brazilian currency and the rapid decline of inflation in the mid-1990s. Estimates suggest that some nineteen million Brazilians moved from the working poor to the lower middle class. For the first time these people had money to spend on consumer goods; those who remained poor also benefitted from stable prices and were better able to afford staples such as meat, chicken, eggs, and beans. Symbols of Social Stratification. Brazilians are preoccupied with class distinctions and are quick to size up the social distance that exists between themselves and others they meet. Yardsticks of such distance are general appearance and the "correctness" of a person's speech. The degree to which an individual's vocabulary and grammar is considered "educated" is used as a measure of schooling and, hence, social class. And this, in turn, establishes patterns of deference and authority between two individuals should they belong to different social strata. When such patterns are ignored, the "elite" persons may harshly demand of their "lessers," "Do you know whom you're talking to?"â€”a ritualized response when someone of higher status is not accorded due deference by someone lower on the social scale.

Classes and Castes. Confucian philosophy endorses a hierarchical class system. At the top of the system are scholars, followed by farmers, artisans, and finally merchants and soldiers. A good deal of social mobility was possible in that system; it was common practice for a family to save its money to invest in the education and advancement of the oldest son. When the communists took control, they overturned this traditional hierarchy, professing the Symbols of Social Stratification. Cars, a rare commodity, are a symbol of high social and economic standing. Comfortable living accommodations with luxuries such as hot running water are another. Many government employees who could not otherwise afford these things get them as perks of the job. As recently as the 1980s, most people dressed in simple dark-colored clothing. Recently, more styles have become available, and brand-name or imitation brand-name American clothes are a marker of prosperity. This style of dress is more common in the cities but is visible in the countryside among the better-off farmers. Many minority groups maintain their traditional attire. Tibetans dress in layers of clothes to protect themselves from the harsh weather. The women wrap their heads in cloth. Uighur women wear long skirts and bright-colored scarves; the men wear embroidered caps.

Classes and Castes. Most national surveys dealing with social strata do not divide the population into different income groups. Instead, the population is categorized into five social layers, according to level of education and occupation. Those social categories are academics, owners of large farms, and persons with more than fifty employees (4 percent); farmers with at least four employees, owners of companies with more than six employees, and college-educated business owners (7 percent); farmers with a maximum of three employees, owners of small companies, and persons with jobs requiring expertise (21 percent); skilled workers, small landowners, and workers with a professional education (37 percent); and workers without skills training (32 percent). In the adult population, there has been an increase in unemployed people who receive public support from 6 percent in 1960 to 25 percent today. Increasing demands for skills in reading, writing, mathematics, computers, and stress management are among the factors that have caused this development. Unemployment rates are somewhat higher among ethnic minorities, with persons of Turkish descent having the highest rate. Figures from 1996 show inequality in income distribution: Twenty percent of the lowest-income families accounted for 6 percent of total income, while 20 percent of the highest-income families accounted for 40 percent of the income. Symbols of Social Stratification. According to a code of morality (the "Jante Law") which was formulated by the author Aksel Sandemose in his 1933 novel A Refugee Crosses His Tracks, a person should not display superiority materially or otherwise. Wealth and high social position are downplayed in public in regard to dress, jewelry, and housing. The point is to be discreet about individual distinction and avoid public boasting while allowing one's wealth to be recognized by persons in a similar economic position.

Classes and Castes. In Egypt there is an enormous gap between the very wealthy and the very poor. The culture also encourages deference of the weak, poor, or subaltern to the rich and powerful, in terms of speech, posture, and acquiescence. The differences among individuals and families in Egypt can be represented by income level or source of income. They can also be represented in choices of consumption styleâ€”housing, transport, dress, language, education, music, and the like. Marriage negotiations bring all these differences of taste and income to the forefront. What is less evident in Egypt is a strong class consciousness that might turn potential classes into real ones. One finds only broad and loose categories that are the subject of much public discussion. The increasing prosperity of Egypt means that the middle class is increasing in relative size, while the gap between the top and the bottom is increasing. One-third of the population is below a poverty line established by the Egyptian government. The growing middle class aspires to a home, a car, and marriage and family life, and increasingly is able to achieve this.

Classes and Castes. There are four major social groups. At the top are high-ranking lineages, followed by low-ranking lineages. Caste groups, which are endogamous, with group membership ascribed by birth and membership associated with concepts of pollution, constitute the third social stratum. Slaves and the descendants of slaves are the lowest social group. This four-tier system is traditional; the contemporary social organization is dynamic, especially in urban areas. In urban society, the division of labor determines social class. Some jobs are esteemed more than others, such as lawyers and federal government employees. Many professions carry negative associations, such as metal workers, leather workers, and potters, who are considered of low status and frequently are isolated from mainstream society. Symbols of Social Stratification. Symbols of social stratification in rural areas include the amount of grain and cattle a person possesses. While the symbols of wealth in urban areas are different, it is still these symbols which index high social status. Wealth is the chief criterion for social stratification, but the amount of education, the neighborhood in which one lives, and the job one holds are also symbols of high or low status. Automobiles are difficult to obtain, and the ownership of a car is a symbol of wealth and high status.

Classes and Castes. France is a class-stratified society whose middle class did not develop significantly until the 1960s. Historically, society was divided among the nobility, the bourgeoisie , the peasants, and the urban proletariat. The French system was the basis for much of Karl Marx's analysis of class struggles during the nineteenth century. The dominant class now is referred to as the bourgeoisie, although this term is difficult to define. Primarily, this class is considered to be the group that controls education and industry. A major source of debate is the issue of social mobility for people of different social origins. Statistics indicate that there is still a strong tendency for children to remain in the occupational class of their parents. For instance, in 1994, almost 50 percent of the children of workers became workers; only 9 percent of them became elite workers. Fifty-six percent of the children of elite workers became elite workers. The school system is blamed for the lack of social mobility. Symbols of Social Stratification. Social stratification has two main axes: urban versus rural and economic class position. The urban upper class generally has ties to provincial seats of power. The bourgeoisie establish the major tenets of good taste and refinement, of being "civilized." One's taste in music, art, food, and leisure activities generally reveals one's social class origins. Symbols of a higher class position include knowing not only about fine art but about the newest trends in avant-garde art, understanding and being able to purchase fine wines, and dressing with understatement while revealing refined aesthetic sensibilities. Class consciousness is very strong. "Symbolic capital" plays a large role in social class, and not only wealth but family connections and lifestyle determine one's social position and opportunities.

Classes and Castes. The systems of social stratification changed significantly because of the increasing income gap between the impoverished masses and former white-collar workers, and the new rich, who have used financial and social capital to accumulate capital through privatization or trade, or have taken advantage of corruption in the state bureaucracy. Another change is linked to the restructuring of the political and economic system from the Soviet centralized type to a free market, although frequently the same Soviet bureaucrats and Communist officials have become capitalists and advocates of a liberal economy. Much of the new capital is concentrated in Tbilisi, Batumi, and the Black Sea port of Poti and thus is dominated by ethnic Georgians. The Armenian and Jewish economic elite that once played an important role, especially in Tbilisi, has lost its position because of emigration or because they maintain a lower profile. Symbols of Social Stratification. An advanced position is expressed by a Westernized lifestyle. A Mercedes car symbolizes success, as do an apartment or house in a prestigious district, summering in France, and sending one's children to private European or American schools. Visiting casinos is another sign of upward social mobility.

Classes and Castes. By the early twentieth century, German society was divided into more or less clear-cut classes. Industrial workers included both the skilled and the unskilled. The former came from the manual trades but became factory laborers when it had proved impossible or undesirable to remain independent. The middle classes included small businessmen and independent members of the trades, white-collar employees, professionals, and civil servants. Finally, the upper or upper middle classes consisted of industrialists, financiers, high government officials, and large landowners, among others. Members of these classes were usually associated with corresponding political organizations and milieus, for example, the workers with unions and socialist or communist organizations, and the middle classes with a range of bourgeois parties, occupational organizations, and patriotic societies. In the German Empire and the Weimar Republic, however, many Catholics of all classes were organized in the Zentrum, or Center Party. The National Socialist (Nazi) Party found supporters among all social classes, especially the middle classes. The Third Reich was, in many ways, an upwardly mobile society, which created new jobs, provided subsidies, and opened career opportunities for party members. The success of many party supporters was gained at the expense of members of the workers' movement and the Jews and other minorities. In the prosperous West German society of the postwar era, class boundaries seemed to open up and admit more members into a new, more inclusive middle class. Correspondingly, the political distinction between the bourgeois and the socialist parties lessened. In East Germany, the Socialist Unity Party wanted to eliminate the bourgeoisie, which had compromised itself through its support of National Socialism. This was to be achieved by nationalizing larger private enterprises, forcing independent farmers to enter agricultural cooperatives, and favoring the children of workers in education and hiring programs. The GDR described itself as a "workers' and peasants' state," but some social scientists have described it as a leveled middle-class Symbols of Social Stratification. Social class in Germany is not only a matter of training, employment, and income but also a style of life, self-understanding, and self-display. The so-called bildungsbÃ¼rgertum, or educated bourgeoisie of the nineteenth and early twentieth centuries, to cite one example, was characterized first and foremost by a particular constellation of artistic and literary taste, habits, and cultural and ethical values. Modern sociologists have tended to focus on the full range of social milieus that make up German society and on the various kinds of consumer behavior that characterize each milieu. Thus, variations in interior design in private residences, eating habits, taste in music and in other entertainment forms, reading materials, personal hygiene and clothing, sexual behavior, and leisure activities can all be viewed as indexes of association with one of a finite set of social milieus. A single example must suffice. Highly educated persons in business, government, or the professions are likely to read the Frankfurter Allgemeine Zeitung (a major newspaper, published in Frankfurt), if they are relatively conservative, or the SÃ¼ddeutsche Zeitung or the Frankfurter Rundschau (newspapers published in Munich and Frankfurt, respectively), if they are somewhat left of center. Members of both groups will probably also read the politically moderate and rather sophisticated weekly newspaper, Die Zeit (Hamburg). Conservatives who are more interested in politics and business than in arts and literature may read Die Welt (another Hamburg newspaper), while readers who identify more closely with an alternative leftist milieu are more likely to reach for die tageszeitung, or taz (a newspaper published in Berlin). In contrast, those with a vocational education, who are small business owners, employees, or workers may read the very popular Bild (Hamburg), a daily tabloid newspaper. Finally, those who still feel loyal to the defunct GDR or who hope that socialism will come again often read Neues Deutschland (Berlin), which was the official organ of the Socialist Unity Party, that is the East German communist party.

Classes and Castes. The caste system is more elaborate than that in any of the other Hindu or Buddhist countries. Society is so fragmented into castes that there can be twenty or thirty distinct castes within a village. This society has a hierarchy of endogamous, birth-ascribed groups, each of which traditionally is Hindu religious theory justifies the division of society into castes, with the unavoidable differences in status and the differential access to power each one has. Hindus usually believe that a soul can have multiple reincarnations and that after the death of the body a soul will be reassigned to another newborn human body or even to an animal one. This reassignment could be to one of a higher caste if the person did good deeds in the previous life or to a lower-status body if the person did bad deeds. The highest category of castes are those people called Brahmins in the Hindu system; they were traditionally priests and intellectuals. Below them in rank were castes called Ksatriya , including especially warriors and rulers. Third in rank were the Vaisyas , castes concerned with trading and land ownership. The fourth-ranking category were the Sudras , primarily farmers. Below these four categories and hardly recognized in the ancient and traditional model, were many castes treated as "untouchable" and traditionally called Pancama . Outside the system altogether were several hundred tribes, with highly varied cultural and subsistence patterns. The whole system was marked not just by extreme differences in status and power but by relative degrees of spiritual purity or pollution. A curious feature of the caste system is that despite its origins in the Hindu theory of fate and reincarnation, caste organization is found among Indian Muslims, Jews, and Christians in modern times. In the Buddhist lands of Korea, Japan, and Tibet, there are rudimentary caste systems, their existence signaled especially by the presence of untouchable social categories. The major cities in modern timesâ€”Bombay (Mumbai), Madras (Chennai), Calcutta (Kolkata), New Delhi, and Bangaloreâ€”were essentially residential creations of the British administrators. Architecturally, professionally, and in other ways, they are therefore the most Westernized cities in India today. In these cities and their suburbs, there is now a developed class system overlying and in many respects displacing the more traditional caste system. As a consequence, there are many modern cases of intercaste marriage in all the cities, although this practice remains almost unthinkable to the great majority of Indians. Symbols of Stratification. There are many symbols of class differentiation because each caste tends to have its own persisting subculture. People's location in this stratification system thus can be gauged accurately according to the way they dress, their personal names, the way they speak a local dialect, the deities they worship, who they are willing to eat with publicly, the location of their housing, and especially their occupations. The combination of all these subcultural features can be a sure sign of where individuals and their families are situated in the caste hierarchy.

Classes and Castes. Iranian society presents a puzzle for most standard social science analysis of social structure. On the one hand there is an out-ward appearance of extensive social stratification. When one peers beneath the surface, however, this impression breaks down almost immediately. In Iran one can never judge a book by its cover. A traditional gentleman in ragged clothes, unshaven, and without any outward trapping of luxury may in fact be very rich, and as powerful as the mightiest government official; or he may be a revered spiritual leader. On the other hand a well-dressed gentleman in an Italian suit driving a fine European car may be mired in debt and openly derided behind his back. Social mobility is also eminently possible in Iran. Clever youths from poor backgrounds may educate themselves, attach themselves to persons of power and authority, and rise quickly in status and wealth. Family connections help here, and hypergamy (marriage into a higher class) for both men and women is very important. High status is precarious in Iran. There is a symbiotic relationship between superior and inferior. Duty is incumbent on the inferior, but the noblesse oblige incumbent on the superior as a condition of maintaining status is often greater, as the last shah, Mohammad Reza Pahlavi, discovered in the Revolution. Nevertheless there are genuinely revered figures in public life. Public respect is largely accorded by diffuse and generalized acclimation, this being a form of status recognition that Iranians trust. The public has a tendency to dismiss awards, promotions, and public accolades as the result of political or social intriguing. The clerical hierarchy in Shi'a Islam is a good model for genuine advancement in social hierarchy because clerics advance through the informal acknowledgment of their peers.

Classes and Castes. There is a vast difference in wealth between the north and the south. There are also the usual social classes that are found in industrial society. Italy has a high unemployment rate, and differences between rich and poor are noticeable. New immigrants stand out since they come from poorer countries. The government has had a vast social welfare network that has been cut in recent years to fit the requirements of the European Union. These budget cuts have fallen on the poorer strata of society. Symbols of Social Stratification. Speech is a social boundary marker in Italy. The more education and "breeding" a person has, the closer that person's speech comes to the national language and differs from a dialect. Style of dress, choice of food and recreation, and other boundary markers also prevail. Clothes from Armani, Versace, and other fashion designers are beyond the reach of the poor. There is a difference also in what food one eats, certain food being more prestigious, such as veal or steak, than others. Although pasta and bread are still staples for all classes, it is what else and in what quantity meat is available that marks social classes. Leisure and the manner in which it is spent are also class boundary markers. The more leisure and the great the amount of travel mark off groups from each other. The more private the beaches, the longer the siesta, the more opulent the family villa, the greater the prestige. Soccer is for everyone, but more expensive entertainment is restricted by cost.

Classes and Castes. Japanese society has been portrayed as being essentially classless or as having a class structure in which very tiny elite groups and underclasses bracket an enormous number of middle-class people. However, there are significant social differences among rural and urban residents, including family composition, educational attainment, and labor force participation. Within the urban population, social differentiation exists between the white-collar, salaried "new middle class," blue-collar industrial workers, and the self-employed petty entrepreneurial classes of shopkeepers and artisans. The neo-Confucian class system was abolished in the 1870s, but remnants of it continue to influence cultural attitudes toward social position, including the entitlement of elite groups to lead society and ideas about conformity to social expectations. Other legacies of premodern stratification include the continued existence of "outcaste" populations. This "untouchable" status results Other marginalized urban social categories include a large floating population of day laborers and migrant laborers, who have been joined by an increasing number of illegal and quasi-legal immigrants from China, Southeast Asia, Latin America, and the Middle East. Symbols of Social Stratification. One of the most important determinants of social stratification is educational attainment. Japanese people refer to a "credential" society, and educational credentials have often been regarded as the most important criteria for employment and marriage, particularly among the urban middle classes.

Classes and Castes. Mexico has a very unequal distribution of wealth, even compared to other Latin American countries. With the introduction of neoliberal economic policies, inequalities have sharpened. In 1998, the top 20 percent of income earners accounted for 55 percent of Mexico's income, while an estimated 27 percent of the population was living below the poverty line. The size of the middle classes has shrunk in recent years. Although poverty and marginalization are widespread, they are particularly strong in central and southern Mexico and especially in rural areas. An official marginalization index that includes income levels and the availability and quality of services (such as drinking water, sewage, and education) indicates that the smallest settlements are the most underprivileged. There is a correlation between socioeconomic hierarchy and ethnicity. Among the poorest segments of the population a strong presence of Indian groups can be found. In 1995, almost all communities whose populations were comprised of more than 40 percent native language speakers suffered from high degrees of marginalization. This strongly contrasts with the wealthiest segments of the Mexican population, which are predominantly made up of whites. Symbols of Social Stratification. Class differences are marked in Mexico and are expressed symbolically in numerous ways. Wealthy Mexicans live in neighborhoods that are sealed off by armed private Wealthy people dress elegantly according to international clothing standards and wear expensive watches and jewelry. Dress codes are very strict in Mexico, especially at work and school. In primary and secondary school, students wear uniforms. Since colonial times, the use of sandals has been associated with the countryside, poverty, and Indians. An important cultural marker of class difference is access to all sorts of private facilities. Whereas wealthy people and members of the upper middle class send their children to private schools and universities, use private means of transportation, and go to private hospitals and sports clubs, the not-so well-off make use of crowded state-subsidized facilities. Class differences are also confirmed in certain behavioral rules. One such rule involves the ritual of waiting that a person from a lower position in the social hierarchy has to endure when seeking access to someone at a higher level. When class differences coincide with ethnic distinctions, discriminatory practices are not unusual.

Classes and Castes. New Zealand no longer is considered a welfare state in which all are equal. Ethnic (Maori, Pacific Islander) poverty is evident in New Zealand has a well-established class society based on income. Cities have developed a "first settler" elite of "old" families claiming prestige and status and occupying the inner ring of the city. Not all are wealthy. Maori maintain a status structure based on mana (inherited or earned) and respect (of older for younger, female for male), though this has largely broken down in the cities. Symbols of Social Stratification. There are ostentatious houses and expensive cars in some areas. The Maori chiefly class ( rangatira ) and chiefs ( ariki ) wear a feathered cloak (as do honored Pakeha) on special marae occasions. Cultural performances of Maori dances include the traditional kilt (male) and apron (female).

Classes and Castes. The highest tier of Nigerian society is made up of wealthy politicians, businessmen, and the educated elite. These people, however, make up only a tiny portion of the Nigerian population. Many Nigerians today suffer under great poverty. The lower classes tend have little chance of breaking from the vicious cycle of poverty. Poor education, lack of opportunities, ill health, corrupt politicians, and lack of even small amounts of wealth for investment all work to keep the lower classes in their place. In some Nigerian ethnic groups there is also a form of caste system that treats certain members of society as pariahs. The criteria for determining who belongs to this lowest caste vary from area to area but can include being a member of a minority group, an inhabitant of a specific village, or a member of a specific family or clan. The Igbo call this lower-caste group Osu. Members of the community will often discourage personal, romantic, and business contact with any member of the Osu group, regardless of an individual's personal merits or characteristics. Because the Osu are designated as untouchable, they often lack political representation, access to basic educational or business opportunities, and general social interaction. This kind of caste system is also found among the Yoruba and the Ibibios. Symbols of Social Stratification. Wealth is the main symbol of social stratification in modern Nigeria, especially in urban areas. While in the past many ethnic groups held hereditary titles and traditional lineage important, money has become the new marker of power and social status. Today the members of the wealthy elite are easily identifiable by their fancy clothing and hairstyles and by their expensive cars and Western-style homes. Those in the elite also tend to have a much better command of English, a reflection of the higher quality of education they have received. Wealth also can be important in marking social boundaries in rural areas. In many ethnic groups, those who have accumulated enough wealth can buy themselves local titles. For example, among the Igbo, a man or a woman who has enough money may claim the title of Ozo. For women, one of the requirements to become an Ozo is to have enough ivory, coral, and other jewelry for the ceremony. The weight of the jewelry can often exceed fifty pounds. Both men and women who want to claim the title must also finance a feast for the entire community.

Classes and Castes. For centuries, the aristocratic and merchant classes were nearly castelike, with endogamous marriage, a strict social hierarchy, and highly codified behaviors. Peasants and serfs constituted a largely impoverished rural population. After emancipation in 1861, as Russia developed slowly along capitalist lines, peasants migrated to factories in urban areas, where they formed an impoverished industrial working class. Strikes and protests and the radicalization of the intelligentsia led to the revolution of 1905, which prompted limited constitutional and social reform along with a reactionary crackdown on political opposition. Widespread destitution, the ravages of World War I, and ineffective political leadership set the stage for the revolutionary activity of February 1917 in which the government was overthrown; this was followed by the political revolution of October 1917, in which the Bolsheviks took power and introduced communist ideology and social transformation. In the civil war of 1917â€“1921 and under Stalin in the 1930s, aristocrats, merchants, and well-off peasants were killed, imprisoned, exiled, or forced to emigrate and their property was confiscated. The Soviet Union was supposed to be ruled by councils (Soviets) formed from the working masses. The creation of social and economic equality was the goal of early communist ideologues. However, Soviet society evolved into a class-stratified and class-conscious state where communist elites and some professionals had special access to goods, services, and housing. Bureaucratic workers and shop clerks used their control of services or goods to benefit themselves through a set of practices known as blat. However, education, health care, and other social services were available to all. Although they had special privileges, most Communist Party officials did not accrue wealth. Postsocialist privatization has allowed many of them to build large fortunes, by parlaying their political status into direct ownership of state resources and industries. A new entrepreneurial class has developed, some of whose members have become fabulously wealthy. More slowly, a middle class is emerging in the cities, formed of intellectuals newly employed in business ventures and midlevel management and service personnel. Most of the population is impoverished, because of industrial collapse, inflation, financial crises, and privatization structures that benefit only the powerful. In 2000, 37 percent of the population lived below the minimum subsistence level of $34 per month. In some regions of Siberia and the Far East, the provision of critical services such as heating, fuel, and water has collapsed. Coal miners and industrial workers have faced severe shortages of critical supplies such as soap, long-term wage arrears, and the collapse of medical clinics and schools. Symbols of Social Stratification. "New Russians" are all presumed to drive late-model Mercedes or Jeeps, live in fancy new red brick dachas, dress in designer clothes, speak on cell phones, and wear heavy gold chains and rings with diamonds. There is some truth to this image, which reflects a popular sense that wealth is vulgar.

Classes and Castes. A major social division is that between guest workers and local citizens. The working class is largely composed of temporary immigrants, who also occupy middle-class positions and a few positions in the upper class. Major variations in income and accumulated wealth exist, with the major categories including the super-rich, the very rich, and the rich alongside a large middle-income group and some with limited incomes. Only small pockets of poverty persist. A strong ideology of egalitarianism is traditional among Saudi Arabians, whose social and verbal patterns of interaction stress equality and siblinghood rather than status differentiation. However, degrees of luxury vary greatly. Differences in lifestyle are increasing as wealthy elites interact less commonly with middle-class people. Common attitudes, beliefs, and practices are shared across economic divides, which also are bridged by ties of kinship and religion.

Classes and Castes. The most important determinants of social status are wealth and education. The basic categories include the wealthy urban educated class, the urban middle class, the urban lower class, the large rural landowner class, and the general rural population. A university education is the minimum qualification for entry into the urban educated class, in which there are numerous substrata. Distinctions can be drawn between the urban upper and urban middle classes. The urban upper class includes several groups with high status determined by education, political influence, and wealth. Wealthy businessmen are accorded very high status, as are successful physicians, cabinet ministers, and many members of the assembly, directors of important government departments, and other high-level officials. Since World War II, businessmen have challenged the old militaryâ€“bureaucratic elite for power and social prestige. Members of the urban upper class are generally westernized; most speak at least one Western language, are well acquainted with European or American life and culture, and have close contact with the diplomatic and foreign business communities. The urban middle class includes most civil servants, proprietors of medium-size businesses and industries, many persons in service occupations, some skilled workers, and university students. These groups usually are less westernized than the upper class and more oriented to Turkish culture. The urban middle class also includes virtually the entire upper strata of the provincial cities. There is considerable mobility within the urban educated class. The urban lower class includes semiskilled and unskilled laborers, low-paid service workers, and the urban unemployed. The high rate of migration of young villagers to urban areas makes this the most rapidly growing class. Many migrants have difficulty finding jobs, and others work only seasonally. Many live in poverty in the shantytowns that ring the major cities. Urbanization continues as the rural population grows and urban industry offers better incomes. Some 30 percent of the population are rural farmers, often referred to as peasants. Improved communications and transportation have brought them into closer contact with towns and cities. Educational efforts since 1923 succeeded in bringing the national literacy level up to 82.3 percent by 1995, although the rural literacy level is lower. Some eastern rural areas are still dominated by large landowners, traditional clan heads, and religious leaders. Young villagers who migrate to towns and cities cannot find their way into the middle class unless they receive further education. Symbols of Social Stratification. Most men of all social classes have adopted Western styles of dress, including trousers, shirts, and jackets. Men and women in the upper and middle urban classes pay attention to Western fashions. They also live in high-priced apartments and try to possess Western luxury items, such as cars, electronic devices, cell phones, and computers. They have developed a taste for Western literature and music and attend musical events and plays. The upper class favors European-language high schools and universities; the middle class is more satisfied with standard Turkish educational institutions. Both classes prefer to speak an educated Istanbul style of standard Turkish. Most members of the lower urban classes live in shantytowns. Only a small proportion have graduated from high school ( lise ). The women tend to wear traditional conservative clothing, including head scarves and long coats, even in the summer. They favor Turkish and Middle Eastern music. The peasant and rural classes are the least exposed to Western and urban influences in dress, styles, language, and music. They, like the lower urban class, tend to speak Turkish with regional accents and grammatical peculiarities. The women wear conservative peasant dress consisting of baggy pantaloons and head scarves.

Classes and Castes. Most Americans do not believe that theirs is a "class" society. There is a strong cultural belief in the reality of equal opportunity and economic mobility. Rags to riches stories abound, and gambling and lotteries are popular. However, there is evidence that mobility in most cases is limited: working-class people tend to stay in the working classes. Moreover, the top 1 percent of the population has made significant gains in wealth in the last few years. Similar gains have not been made by the poorest sectors. In general, it appears that the gap between rich and poor is growing. Symbols of Social Stratification. Stratification is visible in many facets of daily life. The social segregation of blacks and whites in cities mirrors their separation in the labor force. The crumbling housing stock of blacks in the inner cities contrasts with giant homes in gated suburbs all across the country. Speech, manners, and dress also signal class position. With some exceptions, strong regional or Spanish accents are associated with working-class status.

link: Describe, in detail, how social divisions and hierarchies appear within a society, as if you are observing them directly. Capture the atmosphere of daily life that reveals differences in work, status, or lifestyle, and the attitudes that accompany them. Write in a novel-like, observational tone under 200 words, and you may include brief quoted phrases.
text:
In the city square, difference is visible before it is spoken. The merchants arrive first, sleeves rolled, calling out prices that shift with the sun. Behind them, clerks with polished shoes pass swiftly, eyes trained forward, the rhythm of their steps sharper than the chatter of the market. By noon, the scent of roasted grain drifts toward the narrow lanes where laborers rest on stone curbs, their laughter carrying a rough, familiar ease.
Above, on the shaded balconies, women in pale fabrics watch the street below, fans whispering over painted faces. A coachman waits at the corner, hat in hand — the gesture practiced, deferent. Even silence carries rank here: who speaks first, who is answered, who is overheard.
"Some people belong to the sun," a vendor mutters, glancing at the passing carriage, "and some to the dust." Yet the order holds — not by decree, but by habit, by the weight of what has always been. When dusk settles, the square empties layer by layer, each group retreating to its own quarter, the city folding itself neatly back into its invisible lines.

link: Write the opening paragraph of a text that begins with a scene shaped by social position or hierarchy. Let it feel like the start of a larger narrative that reflects how people recognize and navigate distinctions within their society. Keep it under 200 words and you may include short dialogue.
text:
The morning began with the sound of shoes — some clicking, some shuffling — a language of rank that everyone understood. At the factory gate, the foreman’s polished boots struck the pavement with authority, while the workers’ soles, softened by years of wear, moved in quieter rhythm. “Keep the line straight,” he barked, not unkindly but with the confidence of one who had never stood in it. Inside, the air smelled of oil and metal, the scent of necessity.
At noon, when the bell rang, the men with titles took their lunch in a small room with glass windows, while the others sat outside on overturned crates, the same dust settling on all their bread. No one spoke of the difference — they didn’t need to. It was stitched into posture, into the way a man held his spoon or lowered his gaze. Even laughter had its boundaries here, rising and falling in tones that revealed more than words ever could.

link: Describe, in a novel-like but reflective way, how systems of class and social standing operate within a society. Do not focus on particular individuals; instead, narrate the general structure, movement, and perception of difference that define collective life. Keep it under 200 words.
text:
Class in this society moves like a tide — unseen in its pull, yet shaping every shore it touches. It orders the rhythm of days: who wakes to the alarm and who to the knock of a servant, who eats hurriedly before work and who lingers over coffee while reading the news. Streets themselves speak of rank; the cobblestones grow smoother where the wealthy walk, rougher where labor is done.
Status is not declared but performed — in accents, in the patience with which one waits, in the distance kept from those below. Children learn it early, not from books but from glances: which doorway to enter, which silence to keep. Those above move through the world as though gravity is lighter for them, their burdens hidden by design. Those below carry the city’s weight in their hands, its comfort on their backs.
Yet everyone believes the order is natural, as if born from merit or fate. It is this quiet conviction — not law, not force — that keeps the hierarchy intact: a structure built not on stone, but on the steady, unquestioned belief that things have always been this way.

The morning mist clings to the city like a damp shroud, revealing the bones of its order as the sun climbs. On the eastern side, the factory gates groan open, and the first shift pours in—hands calloused, faces set in the grim resolve of those who know their labor is invisible until it breaks. "Another ten hours," one mutters, rubbing his palm where the blister never quite heals. Their boots scuff the pavement, a rhythm of resignation.
Across the river, the cafés are already alive with the clink of porcelain and the murmur of deals being made. Here, the air smells of espresso and polished leather. A woman in a tailored coat taps her phone, her nails manicured to a glossy sheen, while her assistant hovers just out of sight, clutching a tablet and a practiced smile. "The meeting’s at eleven—don’t be late," she says, not looking up. The assistant nods, already calculating how to smooth over the delay.
In the shadows of the high-rises, the street vendors set up their carts, their voices sharp with the urgency of those who must sell or starve. A child darts between them, barefoot, clutching a bundle of newspapers. The vendors shout, laugh, curse—each syllable a claim to space, to existence.
And above it all, the penthouses gleam, silent as tombs. Their occupants sip imported wine on balconies, watching the city pulse below, untouched. The hierarchy isn’t written; it’s breathed.

The grand staircase of the opera house was a river of silk and whispers, each step a silent negotiation of place. At the top, Lady Elspeth adjusted her gloves with deliberate slowness, her diamond bracelet catching the chandelier’s light like a warning. Below, the merchants’ wives clustered in their new velvet, laughing just loudly enough to be noticed—but not too loudly. "Such a pity about the Vosses," one murmured, watching as the banker’s daughter, in last season’s gown, slipped unnoticed toward the shadows.
Near the doors, the footmen stood rigid as statues, their livery crisp, their faces blank. Only their eyes moved, tracking the flow of guests—the way the mayor’s son ignored the clerk’s outstretched hand, the way the widow in black was allowed to linger near the patron’s box, her grief a temporary passport to higher ground.
And then there was the boy with the violins, darting between the carriages outside, his fingers smudged with rosin. He didn’t look up. He knew better. The music would start soon, and the night would unfold exactly as it always did: some on stage, some in the dark, and everyone pretending not to see the strings.

The city moves in layers, each stratum humming with its own rhythm, its own rules. At the highest tiers, the air is thin and polished—deals are struck over lunches that cost more than a month’s rent elsewhere, and voices drop to murmurs, as if volume might betray the fragility of their privilege. Here, power is not spoken; it is assumed, a quiet current that carries decisions downstream without a ripple.
Below, the streets pulse with the labor that keeps the heights afloat. Hands move in unison—stacking, sweeping, sewing—each motion a silent contract with the world above. The language here is louder, sharper, a dialect of necessity. There is no pretense of choice; survival is a craft, and everyone knows their place in the assembly line of daily life.
And then there are the spaces in between, the liminal zones where ambition and exhaustion collide. The shopkeepers who dream of expanding, the clerks who study at night, the children who learn early which doors are open to them and which will always remain shut. The system does not need to announce itself. It is in the way eyes flicker downward in certain company, in the way addresses are exchanged—or withheld. It is the invisible hand that shapes not just what people have, but what they dare to want.

Dawn in the city: the street sweepers in faded blue uniforms push brooms with tired shoulders, their breath misting in the chill. Above them, behind glass towers still dark, executives sip espresso in hushed lobbies, briefcases gleaming like armor. On the corner, a vendor fries *samosas*—his hands quick, his eyes watchful—as a woman in a tailored coat strides past without glancing down. “*Just move along,*” she murmurs, not unkindly, but with the certainty of someone who expects space to clear before her.

In the market, the butcher nods deferentially to the landlord’s wife, wrapping her lamb chops in crisp paper while handing the day-old cuts to the maid waiting behind. Children mirror it all: the private-school boy adjusts his blazer, ignoring the barefoot kids chasing a deflated ball through puddles. No one shouts the hierarchy—it hums beneath greetings, lingers in who holds doors and who ducks through them. Even laughter differs: sharp and bright in the café, weary and low by the bus stop. Everyone knows their place. Most pretend they chose it.

The invitation arrived on thick cream paper, edged in gold—*Miss Elara Voss requests the pleasure…*—and instantly, the whole household held its breath. In the scullery, Marta paused mid-scrub, fingers raw in the soapy water, listening as Mrs. Voss’s voice floated down the hall: “Of course we’ll attend. The Thorne ball is *the* event of the season.” Marta’s own name hadn’t been printed, though she’d stitched the hem of Elara’s gown herself, thread by thread, under lamplight long after midnight. Upstairs, laughter tinkled like crystal; downstairs, the cook muttered, “Pleasure, indeed,” and slammed a pot onto the stove. Marta dried her hands slowly. She knew the unspoken rule: you could be essential to the spectacle and still invisible within it. Later, she’d press her ear to the dumbwaiter shaft just to hear the music, the clink of glasses, the easy way people spoke when they’d never worried where their next meal came from. For now, she returned to the dishes, the invitation’s gilt edge already curling in the damp air—a reminder that some names belong on paper, and others only in the silence between.

Class here is not announced—it is absorbed. From birth, one learns the unspoken grammar of place: which doors are held open, which are pushed through with lowered eyes; which voices carry weight in council halls and which are tuned out in market chatter. Wealth wears quiet fabrics but speaks in deeds—land bought, schools endowed, names etched into stone. Poverty, by contrast, is loud in its need yet muted in its influence, its labor essential but its presence incidental. Mobility exists, yes—but like climbing a greased rope: possible, exhausting, and rare enough to be celebrated as myth. People measure themselves not just by what they own, but by who nods first in greeting, who is invited to weddings, whose child shares a tutor. Status is maintained less by law than by habit, by the thousand daily choices that reinforce who belongs where. And so the structure endures, not because it is rigid, but because everyone, in their own way, helps to polish its invisible rails—some hoping to ascend, others merely praying not to fall.

The divisions are woven into the very fabric of the city. Down by the docks, the air is thick with the scent of brine and sweat, and the dockworkers' faces are etched with the kind of fatigue only heavy, unrelenting toil can carve. Their movements are efficient, their clothes practical, their greetings blunt: "Gotta keep moving." A few streets over, the marble steps of the financial district gleam. Here, the suits glide, speaking in hushed, polished tones about "portfolio performance." They occupy a different rhythm, marked by manicured lawns and the discreet closing of luxury car doors.
Observe the cafe: the waitress, constantly scanning, her smile practiced, and the patron reading a newspaper, calling out "Another espresso, please," without lifting his eyes. It is in these tiny, unacknowledged moments—in the quality of the light that reaches their windows, in the deference or the disregard in their eyes—that the hierarchy lives and breathes.

The sun, already too hot for the hour, beat down on the corrugated iron roofs of the Lower District, a heat the paving stones of the Upper never seemed to absorb. Young Kaelen knew the difference not just in temperature, but in the thickness of the air itself—here, it carried the damp-sour scent of drying fish and too many people living too close. He adjusted the strap of the heavy canvas sack on his shoulder, his hands already raw from the coarse fiber. His task: deliver ten pounds of salted cod to the kitchens of Lord Veridian's estate, a journey that meant crossing the invisible but unyielding border marked by the brass gates. As he approached, a guard in a pristine white uniform narrowed his eyes. "Papers. And stand back from the metal," the man barked, his voice carrying the detached authority Kaelen had learned to recognize as a barrier more solid than the gate itself.

The societal architecture is built upon invisible strata, a layered landscape where elevation is synonymous with ease. It is a system of inherited permissions: a higher class often means access to smoother paths—better education, broader networks, and a general presumption of competence that the lower tiers must constantly earn. Movement between these layers is not a swift ascent but a slow, arduous climb, often requiring the sacrifice of one generation for the meager gain of the next. Difference is signaled not only by wealth, but by the very manner of speech, the cut of cloth, and the unconscious confidence—or persistent anxiety—in one’s bearing.1 These distinctions create an omnipresent social gravity: those at the bottom feel the pull of constraint, while those at the top enjoy an almost weightless freedom, a pervasive yet unspoken order that everyone understands and few successfully defy.

From my café perch, I watch the city breathe its stratified life. Below, men in worn canvas push heavy carts, their movements syncopated by the foreman’s sharp, "Keep it moving!" Their world is grit and muscle.

Across the street, behind polished glass, figures in tailored wool glide between screens, their conversations a low hum of "leveraging assets" and "Q3 projections." Status is measured in the quiet confidence of a suit, the unhurried sip of espresso.

At dusk, the divisions soften into light. The workers vanish into bustling side streets, while the polished ones emerge, their laughter ringing clear as they step into waiting cars, headed for restaurants where a single meal costs a day's labour. The city doesn't just house different lives; it builds the walls between them, brick by invisible brick.

The line for the ration station coiled like a tired serpent through the dusty square, a silent testament to the sun’s relentless climb. Elias kept his eyes fixed on the worn heels of the man in front of him, the collective patience of the queue a heavy mantle. Then, a polished motorcar purred to a halt at the curb, its door opening to release a puff of cool, conditioned air. A man in a crisp linen suit stepped out, bypassing the line entirely with a nod to the official at the door.

“My usual allocation, if you please,” the man said, his voice cutting through the morning’s quiet grind.

A woman near Elias shifted her weight, her sigh a soft, familiar sound of resignation. She didn’t speak the injustice aloud; she simply adjusted her scarf against the growing heat, her gaze lowered. It was a lesson etched not in law, but in the very rhythm of their days.

It is a structure built of invisible walls and silent permissions. Its foundations are laid not in law, but in expectation, its architecture visible in the unspoken map of a city: whose streets are maintained, whose neighborhoods are patrolled, which accents are met with deference and which are dismissed. Movement between its tiers is a rare and arduous alchemy, requiring not just wealth, but the adoption of subtle codes—a particular lexicon, a practiced ease, the right schools. For most, the station of their birth becomes the horizon of their life. The system perpetuates itself through a thousand daily reassurances: that the view from the top is earned, that the struggle at the bottom is deserved. It is a quiet, collective agreement to see the world as a ladder, and to know one's place on its rungs without having to be told.

link: Describe, in detail, how social divisions and hierarchies appear within the Ethiopian society, as if you are observing them directly. Capture the atmosphere of daily life that reveals differences in work, status, or lifestyle, and the attitudes that accompany them. Write in a novel-like, observational tone under 200 words, and you may include brief quoted phrases.
text:
Across a late-morning street in Addis, the layers of society reveal themselves almost without effort. Merchants arrange piles of teff with practiced ease, their voices carrying the steady rhythm of those who work close to the ground. A few steps away, office employees move with a quieter urgency, shoes polished, phones glowing, speaking of “meetings” and “reports” as if naming a different world. In the shade, an older man in a worn gabi watches the flow, his silence marking a lifetime shaped by land, drought, and shifting fortunes.
Hierarchy here is not shouted; it settles into gestures — who steps aside, who is greeted first, who waits. At cafés, you see the subtler markers: the university student ordering macchiato with the confidence of upward motion, the domestic worker entering softly, attentive to unspoken rules. Even laughter carries distinction, rising more freely in some corners than others.
Yet beneath it all lies a shared acknowledgment — an understanding that society is stitched from uneven threads. People navigate these differences with a mix of pride, resignation, and quiet ambition, each day reaffirming where they stand and where they hope to move.

link: Write the opening paragraph of a text set within the Ethiopian society that begins with a scene shaped by social position or hierarchy. Let it feel like the start of a larger narrative that reflects how people recognize and navigate distinctions within their society. Keep it under 200 words and you may include short dialogue.
text: On a hazy afternoon in Addis, the courtyard of the municipal office hummed with quiet sorting — not of papers, but of people. Those in crisp suits clustered near the doorway, speaking in low, assured tones, while farmers from the outskirts waited along the wall, hats in hand, their patience shaped by years of knowing when to step forward and when to stand still. A young clerk emerged, scanning the crowd; his eyes lingered a heartbeat longer on the better-dressed visitors before calling, “Next, please.” The word traveled unevenly through the space, lifting some, passing lightly over others. And in that moment — a shuffle of feet, a bowed head, a lifted chin — the subtle architecture of hierarchy revealed itself, hinting at the deeper story of a society where everyone learns, from early on, how to move within visible and invisible lines.

link: Describe, in a novel-like but reflective way, how systems of class and social standing operate within the Ethiopian society. Do not focus on particular individuals; instead, narrate the general structure, movement, and perception of difference that define collective life. Keep it under 200 words.
text:
Class in Ethiopian society moves like a quiet current beneath daily life — seldom named outright, yet unmistakably felt. It appears in the contrast between concrete high-rises and sprawling informal settlements, in the distance between office towers and open markets, in who travels by car and who waits by the roadside for a shared minibus. People read these distinctions instinctively: the cut of clothing, the ease of speech, the confidence with which someone enters a room.
Social standing is negotiated through layers of work, education, and lineage, each carrying its own weight. Those with formal employment or government ties often stand a little straighter; traders and laborers navigate a world where opportunity shifts with the season; rural families measure status through land, livestock, and the respect of their community.
Yet the boundaries are permeable. Education can lift a household; migration can redraw a family’s prospects; fortune can rise or collapse in a single harvest. And still, the perception of difference endures — a shared understanding that society is tiered, not rigidly, but firmly enough that everyone learns to recognize where they stand, and how to move without disturbing the fragile balance that holds the whole together.

The morning sun spills over Addis Ababa’s cobbled streets, where the rhythm of life is a tapestry of contrasts. At dawn, women in faded shawls kneel by roadside stalls, their hands deftly arranging stacks of injera and spiced coffee beans, the scent mingling with diesel fumes. They call out in Amharic, “Buna! Buna!”—their voices rough with years of haggling, their faces lined with the weight of survival. These are the urban poor, the backbone of the city’s informal economy, their labor invisible yet indispensable.
A few blocks away, the air shifts. Outside glass-fronted cafés, young professionals in tailored suits tap on smartphones, sipping macchiatos priced beyond a street vendor’s weekly earnings. Their laughter is easy, their conversations peppered with English phrases—“Let’s sync on this”—a language of privilege, of education abroad. They move through the city as if it belongs to them, their confidence a quiet assertion of status.
In the shadows, the shoeshine boys dart between cars, their rags worn thin, their eyes too knowing for their age. A wealthy merchant steps from his SUV, tossing a coin without a glance—charity as habit, not connection. The hierarchy is unspoken but absolute: the respect accorded to a landowner, the deference expected from a servant, the way a university graduate’s opinion carries weight while a farmer’s is dismissed with a wave.
At the market, a woman in a crisp white netela—her gold necklace catching the light—bargains sharply with a vendor, her tone implying the vendor should be grateful for her patronage. The vendor smiles, but her grip tightens on the change in her palm. Everyone knows their place. The city hums with this tension, a dance of dignity and disparity, where every glance, every gesture, is a negotiation of power.

The first light of dawn found Alemitu already at the well, her plastic jerrycan scraping against the stone as she lowered it into the dark water. Around her, the other women moved in silence, their wraps pulled tight against the morning chill, their hands rough from years of the same labor. They knew the order: the elders first, then the wives of the shopkeepers, then the rest—like Alemitu, whose family rented a single room at the edge of town. No one needed to say it aloud.
A car horn blared from the road above, and the women paused, watching as a sleek sedan rolled to a stop. The window slid down, and a man in a pressed shirt leaned out, his voice sharp with impatience. “Water for the house—quickly.” He didn’t look at Alemitu as he tossed a crumpled bill onto the ground. She picked it up, her fingers brushing the dirt, and nodded. The other women turned away, their faces carefully blank. It was the way of things.
Alemitu filled a second can, her back aching, but she didn’t complain. She knew her place, just as she knew the weight of the water, the weight of the silence, and the weight of the world that kept them all in line.

In Ethiopia, class is not just a matter of wealth—it is written into the land, the language, the very air. The cities rise in layers: the high-rise apartments of Bole, where the elite sip espresso and discuss investments, their lives measured in foreign currencies and overseas connections; the crumbling concrete of the middle neighborhoods, where civil servants and shopkeepers haggle over school fees and the cost of teff; and the sprawling tin-roofed edges, where day laborers and rural migrants sleep on mats, their dreams as fragile as the walls around them.
Movement between these worlds is rare, but not invisible. A child from the slums might, with luck and patronage, become a clerk; a merchant’s son might study abroad and return with a title. Yet the boundaries remain, enforced by a thousand unspoken rules—the way a man’s suit commands respect, the way a woman’s accent betrays her village, the way a beggar’s outstretched hand is met with averted eyes.
Here, status is not just what you have, but who you know, what you speak, how you pray. The church, the market, the government office—each is a stage where the hierarchy is performed, where deference is currency and pride is a luxury few can afford. The system does not need to be named; it is felt in the weight of a glance, the silence after a request, the way the world tilts, always, toward those who stand above.

In Addis Ababa’s bustling Mercato, the air hums with Amharic barter and the scent of cardamom coffee. A woman in a faded *shamma* kneels by her basket of *kolo*, eyes downcast as a man in a crisp white shirt strides past, phone pressed to his ear—“*Yalachu, this deal is critical*.” Nearby, a shoeshine boy, no older than ten, darts between sedans, his bare feet dodging puddles while uniformed guards lounge outside a gated compound, sipping tea from tiny glasses.  

In the highland village I visited, elders gather under a *sycamore*, their authority unspoken but absolute; younger men wait at the edge of the circle, silent until addressed. A landowner’s wife serves *injera* on spotless enset leaves, while her servant—eyes lowered, movements swift—clears scraps without a word. “*He was born to serve,*” an old farmer mutters, not unkindly, as if reciting the hills themselves.  

Status isn’t shouted here—it’s worn in threadbare cuffs or polished loafers, carried in the tilt of a chin or the curve of a bowed back. And always, beneath the surface, the quiet hum of who belongs where.

The gatekeeper didn’t look up when I approached—just shifted his weight, one hand resting on the rusted chain that barred the driveway to the ambassador’s residence. My threadbare shoes whispered against the gravel, out of place among the glossy sedans idling nearby. “*Name?*” he finally asked, voice flat, already scribbling in a ledger without waiting for my answer.  

I gave it anyway, careful to enunciate the title before my father’s name—*Dejazmach*—though the honorific hadn’t bought bread in two generations. The guard paused, squinted at me as if weighing the dust on my shoulders against the ghost of rank in my voice. A woman in a tailored jilbab swept past us both without breaking stride, nodding curtly to the guard, who touched his cap in reply.  

“You may wait,” he said at last, gesturing toward a bench shaded by a jacaranda. Not inside. Never inside.  

I sat. Behind me, the city thrummed—drivers honked, street boys shouted, the poor moved like currents around the still rocks of the well-born. Every gesture here was a map; every silence, a border.

In Ethiopia, class is rarely named aloud, yet it hums beneath every handshake, seating arrangement, and dialect. Ancient titles—*Ras*, *Dejazmach*, *Balambaras*—linger like faded embroidery on modern suits, their symbolic weight still recognized in the tilt of a head or the deference of a greeting. Urban elites, often fluent in English and Amharic alike, move through gated compounds and private schools, their children groomed for embassies or multinationals, while rural farmers—Amhara, Oromo, Tigrayan—till ancestral plots, their status measured by land, oxen, and lineage rather than bank accounts.  

Yet mobility exists, fragile and hard-won: a university degree, a diaspora relative’s remittance, a successful *khat* stall. These can lift a family into the city’s emerging middle stratum, though old eyes may still whisper, “*He eats with the wrong hand*”—code for unfamiliarity with the unspoken rites of refinement. Religion, too, stitches hierarchy into daily life: Orthodox priests command reverence, Muslim imams guide moral order, and elders—across all groups—hold court as living archives of propriety.  

Everyone knows their place, not because it is fixed, but because the dance of respect—when to step forward, when to bow—is the rhythm by which society holds its shape.

In the bustling markets and the shadowed corridors of power, the social strata of Ethiopia are palpable. The air itself seems weighted with the silent code of hierarchy. One cannot miss the highland Christian elite, whose lineage and connection to the state often grant them access to the best education and most lucrative government posts. Their attire is subtly finer, their manner commanding, and they speak of their positions with an air of inherited right—a quiet assurance that whispers, "We are the established."
Downhill, in the poorer quarters, the difference is stark. Here, you see the historically marginalized groups—the artisans, the tanners, the blacksmiths—often referred to with the dismissive phrase, "They do the dirty work." Their neighborhoods are distinct, their housing simpler, and intermarriage remains rare. The division is not just one of wealth, but of status by occupation passed down through generations. A glance exchanged between a wealthy merchant and a craftsman can hold centuries of unspoken societal distance, a subtle nod that acknowledges the gulf between "us" and "them." The differences are woven into the fabric of daily life—in who stands and who sits, and in the deferential tone one uses when asking, "Who is your father?" The structure feels ancient, ingrained, and rarely questioned aloud.

The sun, a relentless, brassy disc over Addis Ababa, was already driving the shadows to retreat when Kassa approached the gate of the compound. He paused not because the ironwork was intimidating, though it was, but to adjust the white, threadbare collar of his shirt—a gesture that, for him, was less about neatness and more about presenting a plea for consideration. Inside, beyond the bougainvillea spilling over the stone wall, lived Ato Mekonnen, a man who rarely used the honorific "Ato" when addressing his laborers. Kassa took a breath, the dust thick in his throat. A uniformed guard, barely older than Kassa, slid the viewing panel open. "What is your business?" the young man asked, his voice flat, immediately assuming the tone of authority that comes with wearing a crisp, blue uniform. Kassa kept his eyes low. "I have come, with respect, to speak to the master about the delayed pay for the stone hauling," he murmured. The guard sighed, the sound loud and impatient in the morning stillness, a practiced expression of the inconvenience the lower rank invariably caused the higher. "Wait here." The gate did not open. It simply held Kassa outside, a precise boundary separating the one who commands the wall from the one who must wait before it.

The structure of Ethiopian society, when observed collectively, feels less like a ladder and more like a tiered landscape where ancient rivers cut deep valleys. There is a perceptible divide between the established landowning and political class—often associated with certain historical ethnic groups and the orthodox faith—and those whose lineage ties them to manual, marginalized trades.1 Movement between these tiers is not impossible, but it is slow, often requiring the leverage of education or an unexpected political appointment, a change that rarely benefits more than a single family unit.
The collective perception is defined by an ingrained sense of occupational status. To be a tanner or a weaver is not merely a job; it is a label inherited, and the subtle stigma clings to the family name like smoke. The city’s architecture itself reflects this: grand governmental buildings and affluent residential compounds stand physically higher than the clustered, humbler quarters of the laborers, creating a tangible geography of difference. The collective life flows along these channels, where every person instinctively understands their station and the appropriate level of deference owed to those who stand above.

At dawn in Addis, the city stirs. Fresh coffee smoke curls from stone cottages, while the glass towers wait in silence. The divide is in the pace. The day’s first *anbesa* taxi, crammed with workers, groans uphill past a tinted Land Cruiser, its passenger scrolling a screen.

In the Mercato, merchants shout over burlap sacks. “*Bizu! Bizu!*” They move with a weary, practiced haste, hands calloused and dusted with grain. A young woman, polished leather bag held close, picks her way through the mud, her eyes fixed on a middle distance, avoiding the press.

At a pavement tea stall, old men in worn suit jackets debate politics, their voices low and serious. In a garden café nearby, laughter rings over macchiatos; Amharic softens, laced with English phrases—"the board meeting," "abroad next week."

The glance holds it all: the security guard’s deferential nod, met with a slight, unseeing tilt of the head. It’s a city of parallel worlds, each moving to a different clock, each knowing its place in the unspoken hierarchy that hums beneath the daily bustle.

The procession of white robes moved like a slow, sacred river through the packed-earth streets, the scent of frankincense parting the morning crowds. Alem stood pressed against a corrugated metal wall, feeling the grit on his palms, his own faded shirt suddenly coarse. An elder in the procession, his cross-staff gleaming, caught Alem's eye and gestured with a slight, impatient flick of his wrist. "Way, boy," he said, not unkindly, but with the certainty of centuries. Alem ducked aside, his shoulder scraping rust. As the river of white flowed past, his own path—a desperate errand for the *khat* seller who employed him—felt suddenly petty and stained. In that silent command, he recognized the old walls of his world, walls as tangible as the rising heat on the tin roofs above.

The structure is felt, not seen. It rises in tiers like the highlands themselves, a topography of access and exclusion. Birth grants the first language—the mother tongue of the hearth, or the polished tongue of power and pedagogy. Occupation follows, not merely as labor, but as a hereditary silhouette: the scholar's measured gait, the merchant's calculative glance, the farmer's earth-stained resolve. Movement between these plateaus is a celebrated rarity, a story whispered with hope, yet the invisible currents of connection and patronage—*camaraderie*—often prove stronger than merit's lonely climb. Distinction is observed in the dialect of Amharic one commands, in the coffee ceremony's specific porcelain, in whether one navigates the city by crowded minibus or private vehicle. It is a society where ancient lineage and modern capital perform a careful, intricate dance, each acknowledging the other's power. The collective breath holds this knowing—a quiet, universal recognition of place, a map everyone carries, though few would dare to draw it.

link: Describe, in detail, how social divisions and hierarchies appear within the Indian society, as if you are observing them directly. Capture the atmosphere of daily life that reveals differences in work, status, or lifestyle, and the attitudes that accompany them. Write in a novel-like, observational tone under 200 words, and you may include brief quoted phrases.
text:
At first glance, the street looks shared: vendors, office workers, students, pilgrims moving through the same dust and noise. But the layers reveal themselves quickly. Some people walk with time as a cushion—slower steps, phones held loosely, eyes lifted. Others move with urgency, measuring distance by wages and daylight. Uniforms speak softly but clearly: pressed cotton shirts behind glass counters, faded fabric bent over brooms, steel lunch tins carried like quiet clocks.
Work assigns posture. Status settles into accent, into who speaks first and who waits. A guard straightens when a car arrives; a clerk lowers his voice when an English word enters the room. Invitations travel upward more easily than down. Tea is offered differently—porcelain cups here, chipped steel there—accompanied by unspoken rules of seating and address. “Sit, sit,” may be said, but not always meant.
Homes mirror the same grammar: gated buildings with generators humming, a few lanes away rooms stacked tightly, life negotiated by sharing. Respect flows unevenly, yet everyone knows the map. The hierarchy is rarely announced; it is lived, rehearsed daily in gesture, silence, and habit.

link: Write the opening paragraph of a text set within the Indian society that begins with a scene shaped by social position or hierarchy. Let it feel like the start of a larger narrative that reflects how people recognize and navigate distinctions within their society. Keep it under 200 words and you may include short dialogue.
text: The lobby fan turned slowly, stirring warm air that smelled of polish and dust, as people arranged themselves without being told. Those with appointments stood near the counter, files tucked neatly under their arms; others hovered closer to the wall, eyes lowered, papers creased from waiting. When a man in crisp trousers stepped forward, the clerk looked up at once. “Yes, sir?” she said, already reaching for a pen. A moment later, a woman in sandals tried to speak and was met with a raised finger—wait. Chairs filled in a careful order: leather first, then plastic, then the floor where no chairs remained. Nobody questioned it. Even the silence knew where to settle. Outside, a driver leaned against a white car, scrolling idly, while the security guard stayed upright in the sun, counting entrances by habit. In this small, ordinary room, the larger rules were already at work, quietly teaching everyone where they belonged.

link: Describe, in a novel-like but reflective way, how systems of class and social standing operate within the Indian society. Do not focus on particular individuals; instead, narrate the general structure, movement, and perception of difference that define collective life. Keep it under 200 words.
text:
Class in Indian society moves like an undercurrent—rarely announced, always felt. It shapes the rhythm of days: who waits, who is waited for; who bargains, who sets the price. Work is more than labor; it is a visible marker, read instantly in hands, speech, and the confidence with which one occupies space.
